title: Primary Constructors
description: How to properly build a new object from scratch.

slides.title: Default Properties 👎
slides.content:
## Built-in flaws

In the last lesson, every newly created `Car` was automatically Red and going 0 mph.
What if we want to create a Blue car right from the start? It's inefficient to build a Red car, and then immediately paint it Blue.

slides.title: The Primary Constructor 🏗️
slides.content:
A **Constructor** allows us to demand specific information at the very moment the object is created.

We place these requirements in parentheses `()` directly after the class name.

quizData.question: If you define `class User(val name: String)`, what happens if you try to run `val newPlayer = User()`?

options.text: It creates a User with no name.
It crashes. The constructor explicitly demands a String.
It prompts the user to type a name.

quizData.explanation: Constructors set the strict minimum requirements for building the object. If you omit required parts, the object cannot be built.

slides.title: The var/val Secret 🤫
slides.content:
## Kotlin's Magic Trick

Notice how we put `val` directly inside the constructor parentheses `(val color: String)`?

By placing `val` or `var` there, Kotlin automatically takes that value, turns it into a permanent class Property, and assigns it for you. It saves you from writing 5 extra lines of boring code!

quizData.question: If you use `val age: Int` inside a primary constructor, what does that mean for the object later?

options.text: The object's age is permanently set and cannot be changed later.

quizData.explanation: Because it was declared as a `val` (value), it becomes a read-only property strictly bound to whatever the original creator passed in.
